text: CS301 - Data Structures and Algorithms Syllabus Semester 3, Computer Science Department Unit 1: Introduction to Data Structures (2 weeks) - Definition and classification of data structures - Abstract Data Types (ADTs) - Time and Space complexity analysis - Big O, Omega, Theta notations Unit 2: Arrays and Linked Lists (3 weeks) - One-dimensional and multi-dimensional arrays - Singly linked lists - insertion, deletion, traversal - Doubly linked lists - Circular linked lists - Comparison of arrays vs linked lists Unit 3: Stacks and Queues (2 weeks) - Stack operations (push, pop, peek) - Applications: Expression evaluation, Parenthesis matching - Queue operations (enqueue, dequeue) - Circular Queue, Priority Queue - Applications: BFS, Job scheduling Unit 4: Trees (3 weeks) - Binary Trees - properties and types - Binary Search Trees (BST) - Tree traversals (Inorder, Preorder, Postorder) - AVL Trees and balancing - Heap data structure Unit 5: Graphs (2 weeks) - Graph representation (Adjacency Matrix, List) - BFS and DFS traversal - Shortest path algorithms - Minimum Spanning Tree Textbook: "Data Structures and Algorithms in Python" by Goodrich Exam: 30% Internal + 70% End Semester

Algorithm Analysis and Design - Key Concepts 1. TIME COMPLEXITY Time complexity measures how long an algorithm takes to run as input size grows. Common complexities: - O(1): Constant time (array access) - O(log n): Logarithmic (binary search) - O(n): Linear (linear search) - O(n log n): Linearithmic (merge sort, quick sort) - O(n^2): Quadratic (bubble sort) - O(2^n): Exponential (recursive fibonacci) 2. SORTING ALGORITHMS Quick Sort: Divide and conquer, average O(n log n) - Picks a pivot element - Partitions array around pivot - Recursively sorts sub-arrays Merge Sort: Divide and conquer, stable O(n log n) - Divides array into halves - Recursively sorts halves - Merges sorted halves 3. SEARCHING ALGORITHMS Linear Search: O(n), works on unsorted arrays Binary Search: O(log n), requires sorted array 4. RECURSION - Function calls itself - Base case to stop recursion - Used in tree traversals, divide and conquer algorithms

CS301 - Data Structures - Sample Questions 2 MARK QUESTIONS: 1. Define Abstract Data Type (ADT) 2. What is time complexity? Give an example 3. Differentiate between stack and queue 4. What is a binary search tree? 5. Define graph and its types 5 MARK QUESTIONS: 1. Explain the different types of linked lists with diagrams 2. Write an algorithm for binary search and analyze its complexity 3. Explain stack operations with an example of expression evaluation 4. Describe BFS traversal with an example graph 10 MARK QUESTIONS: 1. Explain the various tree traversals with examples 2. Write detailed notes on sorting algorithms 3. Discuss graph representation methods and shortest path algorithms

CS301 - Data Structures and Algorithms Syllabus Semester 3, Computer Science Department Unit 1: Introduction to Data Structures (2 weeks) - Definition and classification of data structures - Abstract Data Types (ADTs) - Time and Space complexity analysis - Big O, Omega, Theta notations Unit 2: Arrays and Linked Lists (3 weeks) - One-dimensional and multi-dimensional arrays - Singly linked lists - insertion, deletion, traversal - Doubly linked lists - Circular linked lists - Comparison of arrays vs linked lists Unit 3: Stacks and Queues (2 weeks) - Stack operations (push, pop, peek) - Applications: Expression evaluation, Parenthesis matching - Queue operations (enqueue, dequeue) - Circular Queue, Priority Queue - Applications: BFS, Job scheduling Unit 4: Trees (3 weeks) - Binary Trees - properties and types - Binary Search Trees (BST) - Tree traversals (Inorder, Preorder, Postorder) - AVL Trees and balancing - Heap data structure Unit 5: Graphs (2 weeks) - Graph representation (Adjacency Matrix, List) - BFS and DFS traversal - Shortest path algorithms - Minimum Spanning Tree Textbook: "Data Structures and Algorithms in Python" by Goodrich Exam: 30% Internal + 70% End Semester

CS301 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS Department of Computer Science - Semester 3 Course Code: CS301 | Credits: 4 | Hours/Week: 5 COURSE OBJECTIVES: - Understand fundamental data structures and their applications - Analyze time and space complexity of algorithms - Design efficient algorithms for problem-solving - Implement data structures using Python/C++ UNIT 1: INTRODUCTION (10 Hours) 1.1 Abstract Data Types (ADTs) 1.2 Time and Space Complexity Analysis 1.3 Asymptotic Notations: Big O, Omega, Theta 1.4 Best, Worst, and Average Case Analysis 1.5 Recursion and Iteration UNIT 2: LINEAR DATA STRUCTURES (12 Hours) 2.1 Arrays: 1D, 2D, Multi-dimensional 2.2 Singly Linked Lists: Creation, Insertion, Deletion, Traversal 2.3 Doubly Linked Lists and Circular Linked Lists 2.4 Stack: Push, Pop, Peek operations 2.5 Applications: Expression Evaluation, Parenthesis Matching 2.6 Queue: Enqueue, Dequeue, Circular Queue 2.7 Priority Queue and Deque UNIT 3: TREES (12 Hours) 3.1 Tree Terminology and Properties 3.2 Binary Trees and Binary Search Trees (BST) 3.3 Tree Traversals: Inorder, Preorder, Postorder 3.4 AVL Trees: Rotations and Balancing 3.5 Heap: Min-Heap and Max-Heap 3.6 Huffman Coding UNIT 4: GRAPHS (10 Hours) 4.1 Graph Representation: Adjacency Matrix, Adjacency List 4.2 Breadth-First Search (BFS) 4.3 Depth-First Search (DFS) 4.4 Shortest Path: Dijkstra's Algorithm 4.5 Minimum Spanning Tree: Prim's and Kruskal's Algorithm UNIT 5: SORTING AND SEARCHING (8 Hours) 5.1 Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, Insertion Sort 5.2 Quick Sort and Merge Sort 5.3 Linear Search and Binary Search 5.4 Hashing: Hash Functions, Collision Resolution TEXTBOOKS: 1. "Data Structures and Algorithms in Python" - Goodrich, Tamassia 2. "Introduction to Algorithms" - Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest REFERENCE BOOKS: 1. "Data Structures Using C" - Reema Thareja 2. "Algorithms" - Robert Sedgewick ASSESSMENT: - Internal Assessment: 30% (Assignments 15% + Mid-term 15%) - End Semester Exam: 70% - Minimum Passing: 40%

CS301 - QUICK REFERENCE GUIDE TIME COMPLEXITIES: Array Access: O(1) | Search: O(n) | Insert: O(n) | Delete: O(n) Linked List Access: O(n) | Search: O(n) | Insert: O(1) | Delete: O(1) Stack Push/Pop: O(1) | Queue Enqueue/Dequeue: O(1) BST: Search O(log n) | Insert O(log n) | Delete O(log n) Binary Search: O(log n) | Linear Search: O(n) Quick Sort: O(n log n) avg | Merge Sort: O(n log n) | Bubble Sort: O(n^2) KEY CONCEPTS: Stack = LIFO (Last In First Out) Queue = FIFO (First In First Out) BST: Left < Parent < Right AVL: Self-balancing BST, balance factor -1, 0, or 1 Hashing: Key -> Hash Function -> Array Index

CS302 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS Department of Computer Science - Semester 3 Course Code: CS302 | Credits: 3 | Hours/Week: 4 UNIT 1: INTRODUCTION TO DBMS (8 Hours) 1.1 Database Concepts and Architecture 1.2 Data Models: Hierarchical, Network, Relational 1.3 Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagrams 1.4 Keys: Primary, Foreign, Candidate, Super Key 1.5 Normalization: 1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF UNIT 2: SQL AND QUERIES (10 Hours) 2.1 SQL Commands: DDL, DML, DCL 2.2 CREATE, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE statements 2.3 SELECT queries with WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING 2.4 JOIN operations: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL 2.5 Aggregate Functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN 2.6 Subqueries and Nested Queries UNIT 3: TRANSACTIONS AND CONCURRENCY (10 Hours) 3.1 ACID Properties 3.2 Transaction States 3.3 Serializability 3.4 Locking Mechanisms 3.5 Deadlock Detection and Prevention UNIT 4: INDEXING AND STORAGE (8 Hours) 4.1 B-Tree and B+ Tree Indexing 4.2 Hash-Based Indexing 4.3 File Organization 4.4 Query Optimization ASSESSMENT: - Internal: 30% (Lab 15% + Mid-term 15%) - External: 70% - Minimum Passing: 40%

Algorithm Analysis and Design - Key Concepts 1. TIME COMPLEXITY Time complexity measures how long an algorithm takes to run as input size grows. Common complexities: - O(1): Constant time (array access) - O(log n): Logarithmic (binary search) - O(n): Linear (linear search) - O(n log n): Linearithmic (merge sort, quick sort) - O(n^2): Quadratic (bubble sort) - O(2^n): Exponential (recursive fibonacci) 2. SORTING ALGORITHMS Quick Sort: Divide and conquer, average O(n log n) - Picks a pivot element - Partitions array around pivot - Recursively sorts sub-arrays Merge Sort: Divide and conquer, stable O(n log n) - Divides array into halves - Recursively sorts halves - Merges sorted halves 3. SEARCHING ALGORITHMS Linear Search: O(n), works on unsorted arrays Binary Search: O(log n), requires sorted array 4. RECURSION - Function calls itself - Base case to stop recursion - Used in tree traversals, divide and conquer algorithms

Database Management Systems - Study Guide Unit 1: Introduction A Database Management System (DBMS) is software that manages databases. It provides an interface between the database and users/applications. Popular DBMS include MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and SQL Server. Key Concepts: 1. Data: Raw facts and figures 2. Database: Organized collection of data 3. DBMS: Software to manage databases 4. Schema: Structure of database ACID Properties: Atomicity: Transaction is all-or-nothing Consistency: Database remains consistent Isolation: Transactions don't interfere Durability: Committed changes persist SQL Joins: INNER JOIN: Returns only matching rows LEFT JOIN: All rows from left table + matches RIGHT JOIN: All rows from right table + matches

LINKED LISTS - COMPLETE STUDY NOTES WHAT IS A LINKED LIST? A linked list is a linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes. Each node contains data and a pointer/reference to the next node. Unlike arrays, linked lists don't store elements in contiguous memory locations. TYPES OF LINKED LISTS: 1. SINGLY LINKED LIST - Each node has data and a pointer to the next node - Last node points to NULL - Only forward traversal is possible Structure: [Data|Next] -> [Data|Next] -> [Data|Next] -> NULL Operations: - Insert at beginning: O(1) - Insert at end: O(n) - Delete from beginning: O(1) - Delete from end: O(n) - Search: O(n) 2. DOUBLY LINKED LIST - Each node has data, pointer to next, AND pointer to previous - Bidirectional traversal possible Structure: NULL <- [Prev|Data|Next] <-> [Prev|Data|Next] <-> [Prev|Data|Next] -> NULL Operations: - Insert at beginning: O(1) - Insert at end: O(1) with tail pointer - Delete from beginning: O(1) - Delete from end: O(1) with tail pointer 3. CIRCULAR LINKED LIST - Last node points back to the first node - No NULL termination - Can traverse entire list from any node ADVANTAGES OVER ARRAYS: - Dynamic size - can grow/shrink - Easy insertion/deletion - No memory wastage DISADVANTAGES: - No random access - Extra memory for pointers - Not cache-friendly TIME COMPLEXITY COMPARISON: Operation Array Linked List Access O(1) O(n) Search O(n) O(n) Insert O(n) O(1) Delete O(n) O(1)

SQL COMMANDS - QUICK REFERENCE GUIDE DDL (Data Definition Language): - CREATE: Creates a new table/database CREATE TABLE students (id INT, name VARCHAR(50), grade CHAR(1)); - ALTER: Modifies existing table structure ALTER TABLE students ADD COLUMN email VARCHAR(100); - DROP: Deletes a table/database DROP TABLE students; - TRUNCATE: Removes all rows from table TRUNCATE TABLE students; DML (Data Manipulation Language): - INSERT: Adds new records INSERT INTO students VALUES (1, 'John', 'A'); INSERT INTO students (id, name) VALUES (2, 'Jane'); - UPDATE: Modifies existing records UPDATE students SET grade = 'B' WHERE id = 1; - DELETE: Removes records DELETE FROM students WHERE id = 2; DQL (Data Query Language): - SELECT: Retrieves data SELECT * FROM students; SELECT name, grade FROM students WHERE grade = 'A'; SELECT COUNT(*) FROM students GROUP BY grade; SELECT * FROM students ORDER BY name ASC; JOINS: - INNER JOIN: Returns matching rows from both tables SELECT * FROM students INNER JOIN courses ON students.course_id = courses.id; - LEFT JOIN: Returns all rows from left table SELECT * FROM students LEFT JOIN courses ON students.course_id = courses.id; - RIGHT JOIN: Returns all rows from right table SELECT * FROM students RIGHT JOIN courses ON students.course_id = courses.id; AGGREGATE FUNCTIONS: - COUNT(): Counts rows - SUM(): Calculates total - AVG(): Calculates average - MAX(): Finds maximum value - MIN(): Finds minimum value Example: SELECT AVG(grade) FROM students WHERE course = 'CS301';

CS301 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS END SEMESTER EXAMINATION - DECEMBER 2023 Duration: 3 Hours | Maximum Marks: 100 PART A (10 x 2 = 20 Marks) Answer ALL questions: 1. Define Abstract Data Type (ADT) with an example. 2. What is the time complexity of binary search? 3. Differentiate between stack and queue. 4. What is a binary search tree? 5. Define collision in hashing. 6. What is the advantage of a doubly linked list over a singly linked list? 7. Write the applications of graph data structure. 8. What is an AVL tree? What are rotations? 9. Define priority queue. 10. What is the difference between BFS and DFS? PART B (5 x 1 = 5 Marks) 11. Explain the different types of linked lists with diagrams. 12. Write an algorithm for Quick Sort and analyze its time complexity. 13. Explain expression evaluation using stack with an example. 14. Describe Prim's algorithm for Minimum Spanning Tree. 15. Explain the different tree traversal techniques with examples. PART C (3 x 10 = 30 Marks) 16. (a) Explain the various collision resolution techniques in hashing in detail. (OR) (b) Write detailed notes on graph representation and shortest path algorithms. 17. (a) Explain AVL trees with rotation operations and examples. (OR) (b) Describe Merge Sort algorithm and derive its time complexity. 18. (a) Write detailed notes on Binary Search Trees with insertion and deletion. (OR) (b) Explain Priority Queue implementation and its applications. PART D (2 x 15 = 30 Marks) 19. Design an algorithm to detect a cycle in a linked list. Explain with example. 20. Implement a stack using two queues. Write the complete pseudo code. BEST OF LUCK!
